The post Leo’s Experimental Micrograntting Program 2026 | $10,000 Grants for Bold Ideas to Help Humanity Navigate AGI appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Have an ambitious idea that could genuinely help humanity navigate the future of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)? Leo’s Experimental Microgranting Program offers a refreshing alternative to traditional funding by providing $10,000 microgrants with minimal bureaucracy and maximum flexibility.

Unlike conventional grants that require lengthy proposals and institutional affiliations, this initiative encourages applicants to spend about an hour explaining a thoughtful idea. The emphasis is on originality, genuine reasoning, and ambitious thinking rather than polished grant-writing or impressive credentials.

Leo’s program is an experimental grantmaking initiative designed to discover unconventional projects that could contribute to ensuring AGI benefits humanity.

About the Program

The Experimental Microgranting Program is built around one simple principle:

Actually try.

Applicants are encouraged to submit thoughtful, ambitious proposals instead of incremental academic work or applications filled with buzzwords.

Leo believes promising ideas can come from anyone and not only professors or established researchers – wants to support projects that traditional grantmakers may overlook.

The program is intentionally lightweight:

  • Approximately one-hour application
  • Maximum 1,000 words
  • Minimal bureaucracy
  • Flexible funding
  • Fast, independent decision-making

Grant Value

Successful applicants receive:

  • $10,000 USD
  • Flexible funding
  • Support for individuals, nonprofits, charities, or other entities
  • Opportunity to reapply after project completion

What Projects Are Being Funded?

The program seeks projects that could positively influence humanity’s future with AGI.

Examples include:

  • AI alignment research
  • AI governance ideas
  • Technical safety tools
  • Educational initiatives
  • Public awareness projects
  • Novel research
  • Policy work
  • Community initiatives
  • Experimental approaches with high upside

Applicants are encouraged to justify how their proposal contributes to helping humanity navigate AGI.

What Makes This Grant Different?

Unlike many traditional grants, this program offers:

  • Anonymous funder
  • Flexible use of funding
  • Learning is considered a successful outcome
  • Supports unconventional ideas
  • Values original thinking over credentials
  • Minimal administrative burden

The initiative itself is also an experiment and may evolve over time.

Application Guidelines

Applicants should carefully read the complete guidance before applying.

Important expectations include:

  • Spend around one hour preparing the application.
  • Write clearly.
  • Explain why the project matters.
  • Avoid exaggerated claims.
  • Avoid unnecessary buzzwords.
  • Submit genuine work.
  • Applications written or edited by AI will be rejected.
  • Multiple ideas may be submitted separately.

Leo specifically emphasizes that applications should reflect the applicant’s own reasoning and writing.

Eligibility

The program welcomes applicants from diverse backgrounds.

Applicants do not need:

  • Previous AI alignment experience
  • Academic publications
  • Prestigious university affiliations
  • Extensive grant-writing experience

Strong ideas and thoughtful reasoning matter far more than credentials.

Funding Philosophy

Rather than expecting every project to succeed, the program views experimentation as valuable.

Even projects that produce negative results may still be considered successful if they generate meaningful learning.

Once grant funds have been used, recipients may apply again by providing a progress update.

Referral Bonus

Applicants who refer others can receive a $100 referral bounty if the referred applicant is awarded a microgrant.

Important Notes

Applicants should note:

  • Grants should not be used as credentials or endorsements.
  • Leo may seek external reviewers.
  • Grant decisions are highly selective.
  • Funding availability is expected to support all successful applications.
  • The program operates independently from Leo’s employer.

How to Apply

Applicants should first read the complete guidance document before completing the application form.

Application Form:
https://tally.so/r/D4WlbR

Questions:
alignmentmicrogrants@gmail.com

Conclusion

Leo’s Experimental Microgranting Program offers a refreshing alternative to traditional grant funding by placing trust in bold ideas, genuine reasoning, and intellectual curiosity rather than lengthy proposals or prestigious credentials. With a straightforward application process and flexible $10,000 funding, the program encourages individuals and organizations to pursue ambitious projects that could contribute to a safer and more beneficial future with Artificial General Intelligence (AGI).

Whether your proposal focuses on AI safety, governance, education, public engagement, technical research, or another innovative approach, this initiative welcomes unconventional thinking and recognizes that meaningful progress often begins with experimental ideas. Applicants are encouraged to write honestly, explain why their project matters, and demonstrate their own original thinking without relying on polished grant-writing or AI-generated content.

If you have a well-considered idea that could help humanity navigate the opportunities and challenges of AGI, this microgrant program provides an excellent opportunity to secure funding and turn that vision into action. Be sure to read the full application guidance carefully before applying, follow all instructions, and submit your proposal through the official application form. Even if your project does not achieve every intended outcome, the program values learning and meaningful insights, making it an ideal opportunity for innovators who are willing to experiment, take thoughtful risks, and contribute to shaping a positive future for humanity.

Discover more opportunities like these on the OFY webpage .

The post Leo’s Experimental Micrograntting Program 2026 | $10,000 Grants for Bold Ideas to Help Humanity Navigate AGI appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth
item.dc:creator: Fridah Kamuri
item.description:
Education Cannot Wait (ECW), the United Nations global fund dedicated to education in emergencies and protracted crises, has officially opened applications for the 2026 ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election Call. This unique global opportunity invites eligible youth-led and student-led organizations to become part of an influential international constituency that helps shape ECW’s […]

The post Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Education Cannot Wait (ECW), the United Nations global fund dedicated to education in emergencies and protracted crises, has officially opened applications for the 2026 ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election Call. This unique global opportunity invites eligible youth-led and student-led organizations to become part of an influential international constituency that helps shape ECW’s governance, policies, and strategic priorities.

Far more than a standard membership opportunity, the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group provides youth organizations with a direct platform to influence global decision-making in education, humanitarian response, and crisis recovery. Selected organizations will have opportunities to contribute to policy consultations, engage with international education stakeholders, vote in governance elections, and even seek election to leadership positions within ECW’s governance structure.

For youth organizations committed to advancing education for children and young people affected by conflict, displacement, disasters, and other crises, this represents an exceptional opportunity to amplify their voices on the global stage while helping shape the future of education in emergencies.

What is the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group?

The ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group is a global constituency established to ensure that the voices, experiences, and priorities of young people and crisis-affected communities are reflected throughout Education Cannot Wait’s governance and decision-making processes.

Recognizing that young people are not merely beneficiaries of education programmes but active partners in designing solutions, ECW created this platform to strengthen youth participation in shaping international education policies and investments.

The Sub-group brings together youth-led and student-led organizations from around the world that are actively working in education, humanitarian response, and crisis contexts. Through this network, members contribute valuable perspectives that influence ECW’s policies, governance, and strategic direction.

Why This Opportunity Matters

Millions of children and young people worldwide face interrupted education because of armed conflict, climate change, forced displacement, political instability, and humanitarian emergencies.

Education Cannot Wait works to ensure that quality education remains accessible during these crises. However, sustainable solutions require meaningful participation from the young people and organizations directly affected by these challenges.

The Youth and Student-led Sub-group ensures that youth voices remain central to discussions about:

  • Education in emergencies.
  • Humanitarian response.
  • Recovery from crises.
  • Inclusive education policies.
  • Long-term resilience.
  • Youth participation in governance.
  • Global education financing.

By joining the Sub-group, organizations become active contributors to international conversations that influence education policy worldwide.

Objectives of the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group

The initiative aims to:

  • Strengthen youth representation within ECW governance.
  • Ensure crisis-affected communities influence policy decisions.
  • Promote meaningful youth participation in international education discussions.
  • Build stronger collaboration between youth organizations and global education partners.
  • Increase accountability through inclusive decision-making.
  • Foster partnerships among youth-led organizations working in emergency education.
  • Support leadership development within the global education sector.

Benefits of Joining the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group

Successful organizations receive access to numerous professional, networking, and governance opportunities.

These include:

1. Participate in Global Policy Consultations

Members contribute directly to consultations regarding ECW’s:

  • Strategic priorities.
  • Policies.
  • Programmes.
  • Governance decisions.
  • Future initiatives.

Their perspectives help ensure that youth experiences remain central to global education planning.

2. Influence International Decision-Making

One of the most valuable aspects of membership is the opportunity to influence international education governance.

Members provide recommendations and insights that shape ECW’s work supporting children and youth affected by crises.

3. Build International Networks

Organizations become part of a worldwide community of professionals and youth organizations working in:

  • Education.
  • Humanitarian assistance.
  • Emergency response.
  • Child protection.
  • Youth leadership.
  • Community development.

These connections encourage collaboration and knowledge sharing across regions.

4. Participate in Governance Elections

Members have voting rights in elections for representatives serving on ECW governance bodies.

This enables organizations to participate in selecting leaders who will represent youth interests within the institution.

5. Opportunity to Seek Leadership Positions

Eligible organizations may not only vote but also stand for election.

During the 2026 membership cycle, organizations may seek election to represent the constituency on:

  • High-Level Steering Group (HLSG).
  • Executive Committee (ExCom).

Successful candidates will serve a two-year mandate, representing youth voices within ECW’s highest governance structures.

Who Can Apply?

Applications are open to eligible organizations that meet ECW’s membership requirements.

Applicants must be:

  • Youth-led organizations.
  • Student-led organizations.
  • Not-for-profit organizations.
  • Organizations working in education.
  • Organizations supporting education in emergencies.
  • Organizations working within humanitarian or crisis settings.

Applicants should carefully review the official Membership & Election Guidance Note for complete eligibility criteria before submitting their application.

Leadership Opportunities Available

Membership also provides organizations with the opportunity to play a leadership role within ECW.

Representatives elected to the High-Level Steering Group or Executive Committee contribute to:

  • Strategic planning.
  • Governance oversight.
  • Representation of youth interests.
  • Policy discussions.
  • Organizational accountability.

These leadership roles help ensure that youth-led organizations have meaningful influence over ECW’s future direction.

Key Dates

Applicants should take note of the important deadlines:

  • Membership applications open: 6 July 2026
  • Deadline for membership applications and election nominations: 26 July 2026
  • Voting period: 3–5 August 2026
  • Announcement of elected representatives: 7 August 2026

How to Apply

Eligible organizations should complete the official application process by following these steps:

  1. Complete the Membership Application Form.
  2. Submit the Election Nomination Form if seeking election.
  3. Download and carefully review the Membership & Election Guidance Note.
  4. Ensure all eligibility requirements are met before submission.
  5. Submit the application before the deadline.

APPLY HERE 

Why Organizations Should Apply

Membership in the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group offers far more than recognition. It provides organizations with a genuine opportunity to shape international education governance while representing the interests of young people affected by crises around the world.

Participating organizations gain access to influential policy discussions, strengthen their international partnerships, contribute to global education solutions, and help ensure that youth perspectives remain central to humanitarian and development responses.

For organizations seeking to expand their impact beyond national borders, strengthen their leadership profile, and influence the future of education in emergencies, this opportunity offers an exceptional platform for meaningful engagement.

Visit HERE to learn more about the Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026

Application Deadline

26 July 2026

Applications and election nominations must be submitted by this deadline.

Conclusion

The Education Cannot Wait Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026 Membership and Election Call is a remarkable opportunity for eligible youth-led and student-led organizations committed to advancing education in emergencies and supporting crisis-affected communities. Through membership, organizations can actively participate in global policy consultations, contribute to ECW’s governance, build international partnerships, vote in leadership elections, and even stand for influential positions on the High-Level Steering Group and Executive Committee. If your organization is passionate about youth leadership, education, and humanitarian action, this is an opportunity to help shape the future of global education governance while ensuring that the voices of young people remain at the heart of decision-making.

Application Deadline: 26 July 2026.

For more global Fellowship opportunities, visit OFY HERE

The post Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group 2026: Apply to Shape Global Education Governance and Represent Crisis-Affected Youth appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals
item.dc:creator: Priyanka Kharbanda
item.description:
The Shorenstein Fellows Program at the Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy, housed within Harvard Kennedy School, is one of the world’s leading fellowship opportunities for professionals and scholars working at the intersection of media, technology, politics, and public policy. For nearly four decades, the program has served as a hub for influential […]

The post Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Shorenstein Fellows Program at the Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy, housed within Harvard Kennedy School, is one of the world’s leading fellowship opportunities for professionals and scholars working at the intersection of media, technology, politics, and public policy.

For nearly four decades, the program has served as a hub for influential thinkers, journalists, policymakers, technologists, filmmakers, creators, and researchers who are shaping how information is produced, distributed, and understood in democratic societies.

Founded in 1986, the fellowship has welcomed more than 300 distinguished fellows from around the world. These fellows have contributed groundbreaking research, influential publications, public discussions, documentaries, policy recommendations, and innovative projects that address some of the most pressing challenges affecting today’s global information ecosystem.

The Shorenstein Fellows Program offers selected participants the opportunity to pursue an independent project while becoming part of Harvard Kennedy School’s vibrant intellectual community. Fellows collaborate with leading scholars and practitioners while producing original work that informs public debate and strengthens democratic institutions.

Whether your expertise lies in journalism, artificial intelligence, technology policy, digital governance, media innovation, political communication, public leadership, or academic research, the Shorenstein Fellows Program provides a prestigious platform to expand your ideas and contribute to global conversations.

About the Shorenstein Center

The Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy is a research center at Harvard Kennedy School dedicated to studying how information shapes societies, governments, and democratic institutions.

Its work focuses on understanding:

  • Journalism and news media.
  • Digital technology.
  • Artificial intelligence.
  • Public policy.
  • Political communication.
  • Information integrity.
  • Democracy.
  • Civic engagement.
  • The evolving global information ecosystem.

Through research, fellowships, publications, events, and collaborations, the Center seeks to improve the creation, distribution, and consumption of trustworthy information worldwide.

About the Shorenstein Fellows Program

The Shorenstein Fellows Program is designed to bring together professionals and scholars from multiple disciplines who are interested in examining critical issues surrounding media, politics, technology, and governance.

The fellowship emphasizes independent scholarship while encouraging collaboration within Harvard’s academic environment.

Participants pursue self-directed projects that contribute meaningful insights into the changing landscape of information, democracy, and public communication.

Throughout the fellowship, fellows engage with faculty, researchers, students, and fellow practitioners while sharing expertise from their own professional backgrounds.

Purpose of the Fellowship

The fellowship aims to:

  • Advance public understanding of media and democracy.
  • Support innovative research on technology and public policy.
  • Encourage interdisciplinary collaboration.
  • Promote thoughtful analysis of today’s information ecosystem.
  • Foster dialogue between practitioners and academics.
  • Produce research that informs policymakers and the general public.
  • Strengthen democratic institutions through better understanding of information flows.

Areas of Focus

The fellowship welcomes projects exploring a broad range of topics, including:

  • Journalism
  • Digital media
  • Artificial intelligence
  • Social media
  • Information integrity
  • Public policy
  • Political communication
  • Democracy
  • Technology governance
  • Election integrity
  • Misinformation and disinformation
  • Civic engagement
  • Digital platforms
  • Media ethics
  • Public interest storytelling
  • Documentary filmmaking
  • Government innovation
  • Internet governance
  • Freedom of expression
  • Data and society

Interdisciplinary proposals that combine multiple fields are particularly encouraged.

What Fellows Do

During the fellowship, participants pursue an independent project while actively engaging with the Shorenstein Center community.

Projects may include:

  • Academic research
  • Policy papers
  • Opinion articles
  • Essays
  • Investigative journalism
  • Reports
  • Webinars
  • Documentary storytelling
  • Multimedia projects
  • Public presentations
  • Books or book chapters
  • Digital publications

The fellowship encourages work that translates complex ideas into accessible content for both policymakers and the general public.

Contribution to the Research Community

Beyond completing their own projects, fellows contribute to the broader intellectual life of the Center by:

  • Participating in discussions.
  • Sharing expertise with faculty and students.
  • Collaborating across disciplines.
  • Presenting research findings.
  • Engaging in seminars and public events.
  • Supporting dialogue around contemporary media and policy challenges.

This collaborative environment allows fellows to benefit from diverse perspectives while expanding the impact of their own work.

Distinguished Fellowship Community

Since its establishment, the Shorenstein Fellows Program has hosted an impressive community of professionals representing journalism, technology, government, academia, filmmaking, and policy.

The Center has welcomed over 300 fellows since 1986, reflecting its longstanding commitment to fostering thought leadership across multiple sectors.

Recent fellows have included:

  • Journalists
  • Documentary filmmakers
  • Artificial intelligence experts
  • Technology leaders
  • Public policy specialists
  • Government officials
  • Academic researchers
  • Digital innovation experts
  • Media executives

This diverse fellowship community creates valuable opportunities for networking, collaboration, and intellectual exchange.

Research and Publications

Fellows contribute to an extensive body of work addressing today’s most important challenges surrounding media and democracy.

Examples of publications include:

  • Research reports
  • Policy papers
  • Commentary articles
  • Explainers
  • Public webinars
  • Essays
  • Original journalism

Recent topics have explored:

  • Artificial intelligence in journalism
  • Media consumption
  • Government technology
  • Digital democracy
  • Information integrity
  • Emerging technologies
  • Public trust

These publications help inform policymakers, researchers, journalists, and the broader public.

Why the Fellowship Is Prestigious

The Shorenstein Fellows Program is internationally respected because it combines:

  • Harvard Kennedy School’s academic excellence.
  • Independent research opportunities.
  • Interdisciplinary collaboration.
  • Public policy engagement.
  • Global visibility.
  • Access to leading experts.
  • Opportunities to influence contemporary debates.

For professionals working in media, technology, journalism, or public policy, the fellowship represents an opportunity to significantly expand both research impact and professional networks.

Benefits of the Shorenstein Fellows Program

Although individual fellowship arrangements may vary, participants generally benefit from:

  • Affiliation with Harvard Kennedy School.
  • Membership in an internationally recognized research community.
  • Opportunities to pursue independent research.
  • Collaboration with distinguished scholars and practitioners.
  • Access to seminars, discussions, and academic events.
  • A platform to publish original work.
  • Increased visibility within media and policy circles.
  • Professional networking opportunities.
  • Intellectual exchange across multiple disciplines.
  • Long-term association with an influential fellowship network.

Who Should Consider Applying?

This fellowship is ideal for professionals who wish to explore important issues affecting today’s information ecosystem.

Strong candidates may include:

  • Journalists
  • Editors
  • Researchers
  • Professors
  • Public policy experts
  • Political advisors
  • Government officials
  • Technology professionals
  • AI researchers
  • Documentary filmmakers
  • Media entrepreneurs
  • Communication specialists
  • Civic technology leaders
  • Digital platform researchers

Apply here

Why This Fellowship Matters Today

The rapid evolution of technology, artificial intelligence, digital media, and political communication has transformed how societies access and evaluate information.

The Shorenstein Fellows Program supports research that addresses these changes while promoting evidence-based dialogue and innovative solutions.

By bringing together experts from diverse backgrounds, the fellowship encourages collaboration on issues that influence democratic governance, journalism, and public trust worldwide.

For more details please visit here 

Application Deadline

No application deadline is provided in the available information.

Prospective applicants should regularly monitor the Shorenstein Center’s official fellowship page for future application announcements, eligibility updates, and submission deadlines.

Conclusion

The Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program is one of the world’s premier fellowships for professionals and scholars dedicated to improving the understanding of media, technology, politics, and public policy. By providing an intellectually rich environment where independent research, interdisciplinary collaboration, and public engagement thrive, the fellowship empowers participants to produce meaningful work with lasting societal impact. With its distinguished global network, longstanding reputation, and commitment to advancing knowledge about today’s evolving information ecosystem, the program offers an exceptional opportunity for journalists, technologists, policymakers, scholars, and creators seeking to shape the future of media and democracy. Interested applicants should monitor the official fellowship announcements for future application deadlines and program updates.

For more fellowship opportunities please visit OFY here

The post Harvard Kennedy School Shorenstein Fellows Program 2026: Prestigious Fellowship for Journalists, Technologists, Policymakers, Scholars, and Media Professionals appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
